title: Agent-as-a-Judge
author: You, Runyang, Cai, Hongru, Zhang, Caiqi, Xu, Qiancheng, Liu, Meng, Yu, Tiezheng, Li, Yongqi, Li, Wenjie
summary: This paper introduces a comprehensive survey of Agent-as-a-Judge, a paradigm shift from LLM-as-a-Judge toward more robust AI evaluation systems. As evaluation tasks grow more complex and specialized, simple LLM-based judgments suffer from inherent biases, shallow reasoning, and lack of real-world verification. The proposed Agent-as-a-Judge framework addresses these limitations by employing agentic capabilities including planning, tool-augmented verification, multi-agent collaboration, and persistent memory. The survey establishes a developmental taxonomy, organizes methodologies across general and professional domains, and identifies frontier research directions, providing a roadmap for next-generation agentic evaluation systems.
content: Computer Science > Computation and Language arXiv:2601.05111 (cs) [Submitted on 8 Jan 2026] Title: Agent-as-a-Judge Authors: Runyang You , Hongru Cai , Caiqi Zhang , Qiancheng Xu , Meng Liu , Tiezheng Yu , Yongqi Li , Wenjie Li View a PDF of the paper titled Agent-as-a-Judge, by Runyang You and 7 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: LLM-as-a-Judge has revolutionized AI evaluation by leveraging large language models for scalable assessments. However, as evaluands become increasingly complex, specialized, and multi-step, the reliability of LLM-as-a-Judge has become constrained by inherent biases, shallow single-pass reasoning, and the inability to verify assessments against real-world observations. This has catalyzed the transition to Agent-as-a-Judge, where agentic judges employ planning, tool-augmented verification, multi-agent collaboration, and persistent memory to enable more robust, verifiable, and nuanced evaluations. Despite the rapid proliferation of agentic evaluation systems, the field lacks a unified framework to navigate this shifting landscape. To bridge this gap, we present the first comprehensive survey tracing this evolution. Specifically, we identify key dimensions that characterize this paradigm shift and establish a developmental taxonomy. We organize core methodologies and survey applications across general and professional domains. Furthermore, we analyze frontier challenges and identify promising research directions, ultimately providing a clear roadmap for the next generation of agentic evaluation. takeaways: Agent-as-a-Judge overcomes LLM-as-a-Judge limitations: biases, shallow reasoning, inability to verify against real-world observations.
Agentic judges employ planning, tool use, multi-agent collaboration, and persistent memory for robust, verifiable evaluations.
First comprehensive survey establishing unified framework and developmental taxonomy for the emerging agentic evaluation landscape.
Methodologies organized across general and professional domains with identified frontier challenges and research directions.

title: Orchestra-o1: Omnimodal Agent Orchestration
author: Zhang, Fan, Zhang, Vireo, Qian, Shengju, Li, Haoxuan, Wu, Hao, Wu, Jinyang, Zhou, Donghao, Zhu, Zhihong, Lian, Zheng, Wang, Xin, Heng, Pheng-Ann
summary: Orchestra-o1 presents an omnimodal agent orchestration framework that extends multi-agent LLM systems to handle heterogeneous modalities (text, image, audio, video) simultaneously. The framework introduces modality-aware task decomposition, online sub-agent specialization, and parallel execution mechanisms. The authors also propose decision-aligned group relative policy optimization (DA-GRPO) for efficient training. Orchestra-o1-8B achieves 10.3% accuracy improvement over the second-best approach on the OmniGAIA benchmark and state-of-the-art performance among open-source omnimodal agents. This addresses a critical gap in multi-agent systems where existing frameworks struggle with complex real-world tasks involving diverse, interacting information sources.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2606.13707 (cs) [Submitted on 10 Jun 2026] Title: Orchestra-o1: Omnimodal Agent Orchestration Authors: Fan Zhang , Vireo Zhang , Shengju Qian , Haoxuan Li , Hao Wu , Jinyang Wu , Donghao Zhou , Zhihong Zhu , Zheng Lian , Xin Wang , Pheng-Ann Heng View a PDF of the paper titled Orchestra-o1: Omnimodal Agent Orchestration, by Fan Zhang and 10 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: The recent success of agent swarms has shifted the paradigm of large language model (LLM)-based agents from single-agent workflows to multi-agent systems, highlighting the importance of agent orchestration for task decomposition and collaboration. However, existing orchestration frameworks are limited to a narrow set of modalities and struggle to generalize to more complex settings where heterogeneous modalities coexist and interact. This limitation becomes particularly pronounced in omnimodal scenarios, where tasks require the unified understanding and coordination of diverse inputs such as text, image, audio, and video. In this work, we propose Orchestra-o1, an omnimodal agent orchestration framework designed to support efficient agent collaboration across multiple modalities. Orchestra-o1 introduces a unified orchestration mechanism that enables modality-aware task decomposition, online sub-agent specialization, and parallel sub-task execution. This scalable design allows agent systems to effectively tackle complex real-world tasks involving heterogeneous information sources, surpassing the second-best approach by 10.3% accuracy on the OmniGAIA benchmark. Furthermore, we introduce decision-aligned group relative policy optimization (DA-GRPO), an efficient agentic reinforcement learning approach for training Orchestra-o1-8B, which also achieves state-of-the-art performance against all existing open-source omnimodal agents. takeaways: Modality-aware task decomposition enables agents to handle text, image, audio, and video inputs simultaneously with specialized sub-agents.
DA-GRPO provides efficient reinforcement learning training for omnimodal agents without requiring extensive labeled data.
Parallel sub-task execution and online specialization improve scalability for complex heterogeneous tasks.
Orchestra-o1-8B achieves state-of-the-art performance on OmniGAIA benchmark with 10.3% accuracy gains.

title: Large Language Model-Driven Cross-Domain Orchestration Using Multi-Agent Workflow
author: Xu, Xiaonan, Chen, Haoshuo, Simsarian, Jesse E., Ryf, Roland, Fontaine, Nicolas K., Mazur, Mikael, Dallachiesa, Lauren, Neilson, David T.
summary: This arXiv paper presents a multi-agent system powered by large language models that orchestrates complex network operations across multiple domains. The system integrates various tools to perform real-time topology retrieval, network optimization using physical models, and robotic fiber switching. The work demonstrates how LLMs can coordinate specialized agents to solve interconnected infrastructure problems that span networking, optimization, and physical automation—relevant for engineers building cross-domain orchestration systems where LLMs serve as a coordination layer between heterogeneous tools and domains.
content: Computer Science > Multiagent Systems arXiv:2410.10831 (cs) [Submitted on 28 Sep 2024] Title: Large Language Model-Driven Cross-Domain Orchestration Using Multi-Agent Workflow Authors: Xiaonan Xu , Haoshuo Chen , Jesse E. Simsarian , Roland Ryf , Nicolas K. Fontaine , Mikael Mazur , Lauren Dallachiesa , David T. Neilson View a PDF of the paper titled Large Language Model-Driven Cross-Domain Orchestration Using Multi-Agent Workflow, by Xiaonan Xu and 7 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: We showcase an application that leverages multiple agents, powered by large language models and integrated tools, to collaboratively solve complex network operation tasks across various domains. The tasks include real-time topology retrieval, network optimization using physical models, and fiber switching facilitated by a robotic arm. takeaways: LLM-driven multi-agent systems can coordinate complex tasks across networking, optimization, and physical domains simultaneously
Tool integration enables agents to execute concrete actions: topology queries, physical model-based optimization, robotic control
Demonstrated application addresses real network operations, not purely theoretical orchestration
Cross-domain coordination requires agents to reason about dependencies and task sequencing
